what: The Convention: Central Body at the Origin
title: Building Two-Body Orbits From Scratch
text: two-body problem, easiest mental model put heavier body origin zero velocity give lighter body interesting initial conditions. works long central body much massive orbiter — good Sun/planet Earth/Moon, good binary stars comparable mass (end). ? velocity whole ballgame. Everything else follows much sideways motion give planet starting distance.
code: system <- create_system() |>   add_body("Star",   mass = 1e30) |>                # heavy; sits at origin   add_body("Planet", mass = 1e24, x = 1e11, vy = ?) # light; on the +x axis

what: Why Velocity Must Be Perpendicular to Position
title: Building Two-Body Orbits From Scratch
text: Gravity always pulls planet radially — straight toward star along line connecting . planet’s velocity component pointing along radial line, orbit won’t symmetric: planet either falling inward climbing outward starting position, closest farthest point path. clean circle symmetric ellipse major axis coordinate plane, want starting velocity perpendicular starting position vector. Put planet +x axis (x = 1e11, y = 0) give purely vertical velocity (vx = 0, vy = v). starting position either closest point (perihelion) farthest point (aphelion) ellipse, depending whether v circular speed. isn’t physics requirement — velocity produces valid orbit — makes geometry much easier reason .

title: Building Two-Body Orbits From Scratch
text: distance rr central mass MM, speed produces perfect circle : vcirc=GMrv_{\text{circ}} = \sqrt{\frac{G \, M}{r}} code: Plug planet’s vy get circle:  ’s . One line math, one call add_body(), stable circular orbit.
code: M    <- 1e30         # central mass (kg) r    <- 1e11         # starting distance (m)  v_circ <- sqrt(gravitational_constant * M / r) v_circ #> [1] 25834.67 create_system() |>   add_body("Star",   mass = M) |>   add_body("Planet", mass = 1e24, x = r, vy = v_circ) |>   simulate_system(time_step = seconds_per_hour, duration = seconds_per_year) |>   plot_orbits()

what: What Happens If You Get the Speed Wrong?
title: Building Two-Body Orbits From Scratch
text: Real orbits almost never perfect circles. interesting question : orbit look like vy isn’t exactly v_circ? Let’s sweep values find .  things notice: exactly v_circ get circle. v_circ, orbit ellipse whose far side still starting distance r, whose near side dips closer star. starting point aphelion (farthest point). v_circ (escape velocity), orbit ellipse whose near side starting distance, far side swings much . starting point perihelion (closest point). 2⋅vcirc\sqrt{2} \cdot v_{\text{circ}}, orbit longer bound — planet escapes parabolic hyperbolic trajectory never comes back. ’ll see next section. “slow” case actually crash star simulation softening = 0 acceleration diverges close approach. ’s one reason unstable-orbits vignette recommends adding softening length anything passes close center.
code: make_sim <- function(v, label) {   create_system() |>     add_body("Star",   mass = M) |>     add_body("Planet", mass = 1e24, x = r, vy = v) |>     simulate_system(time_step = seconds_per_hour, duration = seconds_per_year * 2) |>     filter(id == "Planet") |>     mutate(case = label) }  bind_rows(   make_sim(0.7  * v_circ, "0.7  v_circ  (too slow)"),   make_sim(1.0  * v_circ, "1.0  v_circ  (circle)"),   make_sim(1.2  * v_circ, "1.2  v_circ  (ellipse)"),   make_sim(1.4  * v_circ, "1.4  v_circ  (wider ellipse)") ) |>   ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y, color = case)) +   geom_path(linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_point(x = 0, y = 0, color = "gold", size = 4, inherit.aes = FALSE) +   coord_equal() +   theme_minimal() +   labs(title = "Same starting point, different starting speeds",        color = NULL)

title: Building Two-Body Orbits From Scratch
text: push vy hard enough, planet’s kinetic energy exceeds gravitational binding energy leaves forever. threshold : vesc=2GMr=2⋅vcircv_{\text{esc}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \, G \, M}{r}} = \sqrt{2} \cdot v_{\text{circ}}  Just escape velocity still (stretched) closed ellipse. , trajectory open curve planet never returns.
code: v_esc <- sqrt(2) * v_circ  bind_rows(   make_sim(0.99 * v_esc, "0.99 v_esc  (very elongated ellipse)"),   make_sim(1.00 * v_esc, "1.00 v_esc  (parabolic escape)"),   make_sim(1.10 * v_esc, "1.10 v_esc  (hyperbolic escape)") ) |>   ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y, color = case)) +   geom_path(linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_point(x = 0, y = 0, color = "gold", size = 4, inherit.aes = FALSE) +   coord_equal() +   theme_minimal() +   labs(title = "At escape velocity and above, the orbit opens up",        color = NULL)

what: Which Direction Does the Orbit Go?
title: Building Two-Body Orbits From Scratch
text: sign vy sets direction travel. Starting +x axis: vy > 0 → planet moves +y direction initially → counterclockwise orbit (viewed +z). vy < 0 → planet moves −-y direction initially → clockwise orbit.  traces circle — difference direction travel, becomes visually obvious animation.
code: bind_rows(   make_sim(  v_circ, "vy = +v_circ (counterclockwise)"),   make_sim(- v_circ, "vy = -v_circ (clockwise)") ) |>   ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y, color = case)) +   geom_path(linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_point(x = 0, y = 0, color = "gold", size = 4, inherit.aes = FALSE) +   coord_equal() +   theme_minimal() +   labs(color = NULL)

title: Building Two-Body Orbits From Scratch
text: ’s nothing magical +x axis. ’d rather place planet +y axis, just swap: position goes y, velocity goes vx (opposite sign convention direction).  circle, rotated 90 degrees. can place planet anywhere plane, long velocity vector perpendicular position vector magnitude v_circ (circle) deliberate deviation (ellipse).
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Star",   mass = M) |>   add_body("Planet", mass = 1e24, y = r, vx = -v_circ) |>   simulate_system(time_step = seconds_per_hour, duration = seconds_per_year) |>   plot_orbits()

what: When the Central Body Isn’t Much Heavier
title: Building Two-Body Orbits From Scratch
text: Everything assumed star heavy doesn’t move appreciably. ’s fine approximation mass ratio ≳104\gtrsim 10^4 (Sun/Earth ∼3×105\sim 3 \times 10^5), breaks binary stars, Pluto-Charon, system two masses comparable. , bodies orbit common center mass (barycenter), need give bodies initial velocities satisfy conservation momentum. Kepler-16 example shows realistic binary-star-plus-planet system. short version: Put two bodies opposite sides origin distances inversely proportional masses. Give initial velocity opposite directions, inversely proportional mass. orbital speed body around barycenter uses body’s mass total separation. anything resembling planet around much heavier star, single-body approach guide need.

title: Building Two-Body Orbits From Scratch
text: build stable two-body orbit scratch: Pick central mass MM place origin zero velocity. Pick starting distance rr orbiter place one axis (e.g. x = r, y = 0, z = 0). Compute vcirc=GM/rv_{\text{circ}} = \sqrt{GM/r}. Give orbiter velocity perpendicular position vector (e.g. vy = v_circ, vx = 0). Use exactly v_circ circle, less inward ellipse, outward ellipse, sqrt(2) * v_circ escape trajectory. ’s entire recipe. Every built-example orbitr — every custom system build — boils applying four steps one pair bodies time.

title: Custom Visualization with ggplot2 and plotly
text: ’s simulation tibble looks like: row one body one point time. Every column available plotting, filtering, analysis. Since just tibble, full power dplyr ggplot2 disposal.

title: Custom Visualization with ggplot2 and plotly
text: example, Earth-Moon system, plot_orbits() shows overlapping circles bodies orbit shared barycenter roughly scale. useful visualization might plot body’s distance barycenter time:  plot Moon’s path relative Earth color gradient showing passage time:
code: library(ggplot2)  sim |>   dplyr::mutate(r = sqrt(x^2 + y^2)) |>   ggplot(aes(x = time / seconds_per_day, y = r, color = id)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1) +   labs(     title = "Distance from Barycenter Over Time",     x = "Time (days)",     y = "Distance (m)",     color = "Body"   ) +   theme_minimal() sim |>   shift_reference_frame("Earth", keep_center = FALSE) |>   ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y, color = time / seconds_per_day)) +   geom_path(linewidth = 1.2) +   scale_color_viridis_c(name = "Day") +   coord_equal() +   labs(title = "Lunar Orbit (Earth-Centered)", x = "X (m)", y = "Y (m)") +   theme_minimal()

title: Custom Visualization with ggplot2 and plotly
text: Just plot_orbits() quick convenience 2D work, plot_orbits_3d() quick convenience 3D. intentionally simple — get useful plot one line can focus physics, formatting. need control, simulation tibble works just well plotly ggplot2. example, color trajectories speed rather body: point ggplot2: simulate_system() returns standard tibble, full access plotly’s API anything built-plotting functions don’t cover.
code: library(plotly) #>  #> Attaching package: 'plotly' #> The following object is masked from 'package:ggplot2': #>  #>     last_plot #> The following object is masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter #> The following object is masked from 'package:graphics': #>  #>     layout  sim <- create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon,            x = distance_earth_moon,            vy = speed_moon * cos(5 * pi / 180),            vz = speed_moon * sin(5 * pi / 180)) |>   simulate_system(time_step = seconds_per_hour, duration = seconds_per_day * 28)  sim <- sim |>   dplyr::mutate(speed = sqrt(vx^2 + vy^2 + vz^2))  plot_ly() |>   add_trace(     data = dplyr::filter(sim, id == "Moon"),     x = ~x, y = ~y, z = ~z,     type = 'scatter3d', mode = 'lines',     line = list(       width = 5,       color = ~speed,       colorscale = 'Viridis',       showscale = TRUE,       colorbar = list(title = "Speed (m/s)")     ),     name = "Moon"   ) |>   add_trace(     data = dplyr::filter(sim, id == "Earth"),     x = ~x, y = ~y, z = ~z,     type = 'scatter3d', mode = 'lines',     line = list(width = 3, color = 'gray'),     name = "Earth"   ) |>   layout(     title = "Lunar Orbit Around Earth",     showlegend = FALSE,     scene = list(       xaxis = list(title = 'X (m)'),       yaxis = list(title = 'Y (m)'),       zaxis = list(title = 'Z (m)'),       aspectmode = "data"     )   )

title: Examples
text: fastest way get complete solar system simulation:  planets start periapsis (closest point Sun) default, real eccentricities, inclinations, orbital orientations JPL. inner planets complete orbits quickly outer planets barely move single year.
code: solar <- load_solar_system() |>   simulate_system(time_step = seconds_per_day, duration = seconds_per_year)  solar |> plot_orbits(three_d = FALSE)

title: Examples
text: Use add_planet() pick specific bodies without looking numbers:  Notice Mercury’s orbit visibly eccentric compared near-circular orbits Venus Earth. Mars also shows elongation.
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Sun", mass = mass_sun) |>   add_planet("Mercury", parent = "Sun") |>   add_planet("Venus",   parent = "Sun") |>   add_planet("Earth",   parent = "Sun") |>   add_planet("Mars",    parent = "Sun") |>   simulate_system(time_step = seconds_per_day, duration = seconds_per_year * 2) |>   plot_orbits(three_d = FALSE)

code: earth_moon <- create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth) |>   add_planet("Moon", parent = "Earth") |>   simulate_system(time_step = seconds_per_hour, duration = seconds_per_day * 28)  earth_moon |> plot_orbits() animate_system(earth_moon, fps = 15, duration = 5)

title: Examples
text: orbitr uses N-body gravity, nested hierarchies require special setup. Piggyback Moon’s initial conditions onto Earth’s using simple vector addition. Note scale, Earth Moon orbits overlap — Earth-Moon distance (~384,000 km) tiny compared Earth-Sun distance (~150 million km). Use shift_reference_frame("Earth") (shown next example) zoom Earth-Moon subsystem:
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Sun",   mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth, x = distance_earth_sun, vy = speed_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon,            x = distance_earth_sun + distance_earth_moon,            vy = speed_earth + speed_moon) |>   simulate_system(time_step = seconds_per_hour, duration = seconds_per_year) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Examples
text: three-body plot heliocentric (Sun center). see Moon’s path Earth’s perspective, pipe results shift_reference_frame():  Animating Earth-frame view makes Moon’s monthly loops around Earth obvious Sun drifts across background:
code: sun_earth_moon <- create_system() |>   add_body("Sun",   mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth, x = distance_earth_sun, vy = speed_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon,            x = distance_earth_sun + distance_earth_moon,            vy = speed_earth + speed_moon) |>   simulate_system(time_step = seconds_per_hour, duration = seconds_per_year) |>   shift_reference_frame("Earth")  sun_earth_moon |> plot_orbits() animate_system(sun_earth_moon, fps = 15, duration = 6)

what: The Kepler-16 System: A Real Circumbinary Planet
title: Examples
text: Kepler-16b first confirmed planet orbiting two stars — real-life Tatooine. system K-type star (0.68 solar masses) M-type star (0.20 solar masses) orbiting every ~41 days, Saturn-sized planet orbiting pair 0.7 AU.  animation makes circumbinary structure pop — two stars whirl tightly around common center planet traces much wider, slower loop around pair:
code: AU <- distance_earth_sun  # Star masses m_A <- 0.68 * mass_sun m_B <- 0.20 * mass_sun m_planet <- 0.333 * mass_jupiter  # Binary star orbit (~0.22 AU separation) a_bin <- 0.22 * AU r_A <- a_bin * m_B / (m_A + m_B) r_B <- a_bin * m_A / (m_A + m_B) v_A <- sqrt(gravitational_constant * m_B^2 / ((m_A + m_B) * a_bin)) v_B <- sqrt(gravitational_constant * m_A^2 / ((m_A + m_B) * a_bin))  # Planet orbit (0.7048 AU from barycenter) r_planet <- 0.7048 * AU v_planet <- sqrt(gravitational_constant * (m_A + m_B) / r_planet)  kepler16 <- create_system() |>   add_body("Star A", mass = m_A, x = r_A, vy = v_A) |>   add_body("Star B", mass = m_B, x = -r_B, vy = -v_B) |>   add_body("Kepler-16b", mass = m_planet, x = r_planet, vy = v_planet) |>   simulate_system(time_step = seconds_per_hour, duration = seconds_per_day * 228.8 * 3)  kepler16 |> plot_orbits() animate_system(kepler16, fps = 15, duration = 6)

what: A Comet Crossing the Inner Solar System
title: Examples
text: add_body_keplerian() shines objects extreme orbits. ’s Halley-like comet highly eccentric, steeply inclined orbit plunges inner solar system: comet’s orbit tilted 50° ecliptic eccentricity 0.85, meaning farthest distance Sun 12 times closest approach. Starting ν=150°\nu = 150° places near apoapsis, heading inward.
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Sun", mass = mass_sun) |>   add_planet("Earth", parent = "Sun") |>   add_body_keplerian(     "Comet", mass = 2.2e14, parent = "Sun",     a = 2.5 * distance_earth_sun, e = 0.85,     i = 50, lan = 60, arg_pe = 120, nu = 150   ) |>   simulate_system(time_step = seconds_per_hour * 6, duration = seconds_per_year * 3) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Examples
text: One nice things add_planet() can override individual elements keeping everything else real. Mars perfectly circular orbit?
code: bind_rows(   create_system() |>     add_body("Sun", mass = mass_sun) |>     add_planet("Mars", parent = "Sun") |>     simulate_system(time_step = seconds_per_day,                     duration = seconds_per_day * 687) |>     filter(id == "Mars") |>     mutate(case = "Real Mars (e = 0.093)"),   create_system() |>     add_body("Sun", mass = mass_sun) |>     add_planet("Mars", parent = "Sun", e = 0) |>     simulate_system(time_step = seconds_per_day,                     duration = seconds_per_day * 687) |>     filter(id == "Mars") |>     mutate(case = "Circular Mars (e = 0)") ) |>   ggplot2::ggplot(ggplot2::aes(x = x, y = y, color = case)) +   ggplot2::geom_path(linewidth = 0.8) +   ggplot2::coord_equal() +   ggplot2::theme_minimal() +   ggplot2::labs(color = NULL)

title: Keplerian Orbital Elements
text: orbit three-dimensional space six degrees freedom — number Cartesian state vector (x, y, z, vx, vy, vz). Keplerian elements split six numbers three intuitive groups: shape, orientation, position.

title: Keplerian Orbital Elements
text:
first two elements define geometry ellipse — big stretched. Semi-major axis (aa) average distance orbiting body parent. Technically ’s half longest diameter ellipse. Larger aa means bigger orbit longer period. orbitr, ’s specified meters. Eccentricity (ee) controls shape. circle e=0e = 0; ee increases toward 1, ellipse becomes elongated. point along orbit, actual distance parent : r=(1−e2)1+ecosνr =  rac{(1 - e^2)}{1 + e \cos
u} ν
u true anomaly (body’s current angular position — ). closest approach (periapsis, ν=0
u = 0), distance rperi=(1−e)r_{	ext{peri}} = (1-e). farthest point (apoapsis, ν=180°
u = 180°), ’s rapo=(1+e)r_{	ext{apo}} = (1+e). Let’s see eccentricity changes orbit shape:  four orbits semi-major axis, orbital period (Kepler’s Third Law: T2∝a3T^2 \propto ^3). shapes radically different — perfect circle narrow ellipse barely misses Sun periapsis swings far apoapsis.
code: make_ecc_orbit <- function(e, label) {   create_system() |>     add_body("Sun", mass = mass_sun) |>     add_body_keplerian(       "Planet", mass = 1e24, parent = "Sun",       a = distance_earth_sun, e = e, nu = 0     ) |>     simulate_system(time_step = seconds_per_day, duration = seconds_per_year * 2) |>     filter(id == "Planet") |>     mutate(case = label) }  bind_rows(   make_ecc_orbit(0.0,  "e = 0.0 (circle)"),   make_ecc_orbit(0.2,  "e = 0.2 (Mercury-like)"),   make_ecc_orbit(0.5,  "e = 0.5 (comet-like)"),   make_ecc_orbit(0.85, "e = 0.85 (Halley-like)") ) |>   ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y, color = case)) +   geom_path(linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_point(x = 0, y = 0, color = "gold", size = 4, inherit.aes = FALSE) +   coord_equal() +   theme_minimal() +   labs(title = "Same semi-major axis, different eccentricities",        color = NULL)

what: Orientation: Inclination, Longitude of Ascending Node, and Argument of Periapsis
title: Keplerian Orbital Elements
text: next three elements rotate tilt ellipse 3D space. ’re specified degrees. Inclination (ii) tilt orbital plane relative reference plane (typically ecliptic — plane Earth’s orbit). inclination 0°0° means orbit lies flat reference plane. 90°90° means orbit perpendicular .  flat orbit (=0i = 0) stays XY plane z=0z = 0. inclination increases, orbit tilts plane =90°= 90° makes full polar orbit. Longitude ascending node (Ω\Omega, called lan orbitr) compass direction tilt. measures angle reference direction (vernal equinox real astronomy) point orbit crosses reference plane going “upward.” Think inclination much orbit tilts, Ω\Omega direction tilts toward. =0i = 0, ascending node undefined Ω\Omega effect.  three orbits shape inclination — Ω\Omega just rotates whole tilted plane around vertical axis. Argument periapsis (ω\omega, called arg_pe orbitr) angle within orbital plane ascending node closest-approach point (periapsis). controls along orbit body gets closest parent.  ellipse shape every time — ω\omega just spins around parent, moving periapsis different points along orbit.
code: make_inc_orbit <- function(inc, label) {   create_system() |>     add_body("Sun", mass = mass_sun) |>     add_body_keplerian(       "Planet", mass = 1e24, parent = "Sun",       a = distance_earth_sun, e = 0.1, i = inc, nu = 0     ) |>     simulate_system(time_step = seconds_per_day, duration = seconds_per_year) |>     filter(id == "Planet") |>     mutate(case = label) }  bind_rows(   make_inc_orbit(0,  "i = 0 (flat)"),   make_inc_orbit(30, "i = 30"),   make_inc_orbit(60, "i = 60"),   make_inc_orbit(90, "i = 90 (polar)") ) |>   ggplot(aes(x = x, y = z, color = case)) +   geom_path(linewidth = 0.8) +   coord_equal() +   theme_minimal() +   labs(title = "Same orbit, different inclinations (viewed from the side: X vs Z)",        x = "X (m)", y = "Z (m)", color = NULL) make_lan_orbit <- function(lan_val, label) {   create_system() |>     add_body("Sun", mass = mass_sun) |>     add_body_keplerian(       "Planet", mass = 1e24, parent = "Sun",       a = distance_earth_sun, e = 0.1, i = 45, lan = lan_val, nu = 0     ) |>     simulate_system(time_step = seconds_per_day, duration = seconds_per_year) |>     filter(id == "Planet") |>     mutate(case = label) }  bind_rows(   make_lan_orbit(0,   "LAN = 0"),   make_lan_orbit(90,  "LAN = 90"),   make_lan_orbit(180, "LAN = 180") ) |>   ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y, color = case)) +   geom_path(linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_point(x = 0, y = 0, color = "gold", size = 4, inherit.aes = FALSE) +   coord_equal() +   theme_minimal() +   labs(title = "Same orbit tilted in different directions (top-down view)",        color = NULL) make_argpe_orbit <- function(argpe_val, label) {   create_system() |>     add_body("Sun", mass = mass_sun) |>     add_body_keplerian(       "Planet", mass = 1e24, parent = "Sun",       a = distance_earth_sun, e = 0.5, arg_pe = argpe_val, nu = 0     ) |>     simulate_system(time_step = seconds_per_day, duration = seconds_per_year * 2) |>     filter(id == "Planet") |>     mutate(case = label) }  bind_rows(   make_argpe_orbit(0,   "arg_pe = 0"),   make_argpe_orbit(90,  "arg_pe = 90"),   make_argpe_orbit(180, "arg_pe = 180") ) |>   ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y, color = case)) +   geom_path(linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_point(x = 0, y = 0, color = "gold", size = 4, inherit.aes = FALSE) +   coord_equal() +   theme_minimal() +   labs(title = "Same ellipse, rotated within the orbital plane",        color = NULL)

title: Keplerian Orbital Elements
text:
final element pins body currently along orbit. True anomaly (ν
u, called nu orbitr) angle measured periapsis body’s current position, direction orbital motion. ν=0°
u = 0° means body starts periapsis (closest point); ν=180°
u = 180° puts apoapsis (farthest point).  default, add_body_keplerian() add_planet() use ν=0
u = 0 (starting periapsis). load_solar_system(), planets start periapsis — want spread around orbits realistic snapshot, pass different nu values add_planet():
code: sys_0 <- create_system() |>   add_body("Sun", mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body_keplerian("Planet", mass = 1e24, parent = "Sun",                       a = distance_earth_sun, e = 0.3, nu = 0)  sys_90 <- create_system() |>   add_body("Sun", mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body_keplerian("Planet", mass = 1e24, parent = "Sun",                       a = distance_earth_sun, e = 0.3, nu = 90)  sys_180 <- create_system() |>   add_body("Sun", mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body_keplerian("Planet", mass = 1e24, parent = "Sun",                       a = distance_earth_sun, e = 0.3, nu = 180)  # Plot starting positions starts <- bind_rows(   sys_0$bodies |> filter(id == "Planet") |> mutate(case = "nu = 0 (periapsis)"),   sys_90$bodies |> filter(id == "Planet") |> mutate(case = "nu = 90"),   sys_180$bodies |> filter(id == "Planet") |> mutate(case = "nu = 180 (apoapsis)") )  # Full orbit for reference orbit <- simulate_system(sys_0, time_step = seconds_per_day,                          duration = seconds_per_year * 2) |>   filter(id == "Planet")  ggplot() +   geom_path(data = orbit, aes(x = x, y = y), color = "grey70", linewidth = 0.5) +   geom_point(data = starts, aes(x = x, y = y, color = case), size = 4) +   geom_point(x = 0, y = 0, color = "gold", size = 4) +   coord_equal() +   theme_minimal() +   labs(title = "Starting position at different true anomaly values",        color = NULL) # Spread the inner planets around their orbits create_system() |>   add_body("Sun", mass = mass_sun) |>   add_planet("Mercury", parent = "Sun", nu = 30) |>   add_planet("Venus",   parent = "Sun", nu = 120) |>   add_planet("Earth",   parent = "Sun", nu = 210) |>   add_planet("Mars",    parent = "Sun", nu = 300) |>   simulate_system(time_step = seconds_per_day, duration = seconds_per_year) |>   plot_orbits(three_d = FALSE)

title: Keplerian Orbital Elements
text:
hood, add_body_keplerian() converts six elements Cartesian state vector (position velocity) three-step process. Understanding isn’t necessary using function, ’s useful want verify math extend . Step 1: Position velocity orbital plane. orbit equation gives distance current true anomaly: r=(1−e2)1+ecosνr =  rac{(1-e^2)}{1 + e\cos
u} Position perifocal frame (coordinate system aligned orbit, x-axis pointing toward periapsis): xpf=rcosν,ypf=rsinνx_{	ext{pf}} = r\cos
u, \quad y_{	ext{pf}} = r\sin
u specific angular momentum h=μa(1−e2)h = \sqrt{\mu (1-e^2)} (μ=GMparent\mu = GM_{	ext{parent}}) gives velocity: vx,pf=−μhsinν,vy,pf=μh(e+cosν)v_{x,	ext{pf}} = - rac{\mu}{h}\sin
u, \quad v_{y,	ext{pf}} =  rac{\mu}{h}(e + \cos
u) Step 2: Rotate inertial coordinates. Three successive rotations — −ω-\omega around orbit normal, −-around node line, −Ω-\Omega around reference pole — transform perifocal frame inertial reference frame. Step 3: Add parent’s state. resulting position velocity relative parent body. add_body_keplerian() adds parent’s current position velocity produce absolute coordinates simulation frame.

title: Reference Frames
text: Every N-body simulation pick origin — point sits (0, 0, 0). default, orbitr uses coordinate system set : whatever body placed origin stays (least initially), everything else measured relative point. reference frame. trouble “natural” reference frame building simulation isn’t always best one understanding results. Consider Sun-Earth-Moon system. obvious way set heliocentric (Sun origin):  scale Earth-Moon distance (~384,000 km) rounding error compared Earth-Sun distance (~150 million km). Moon’s trajectory overlaps Earth’s completely, Sun barely moves (tiny wobble around barycenter invisible zoom level), plot_orbits() shows looks like single circular track. physics fine; perspective wrong.
code: sim <- create_system() |>   add_body("Sun",   mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth, x = distance_earth_sun, vy = speed_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon,            x = distance_earth_sun + distance_earth_moon,            vy = speed_earth + speed_moon) |>   simulate_system(time_step = seconds_per_hour, duration = seconds_per_year)  sim |> plot_orbits()

title: Reference Frames
text: shift_reference_frame() fixes applying Galilean coordinate transformation. every time step, subtracts position velocity chosen body bodies: r→′(t)=r→(t)−r→center(t)v→′(t)=v→(t)−v→center(t) \vec{r}'_i(t) = \vec{r}_i(t) - \vec{r}_{\text{center}}(t) \qquad \vec{v}'_i(t) = \vec{v}_i(t) - \vec{v}_{\text{center}}(t) chosen body ends fixed origin, every body’s trajectory shows motion relative body. physics changes — forces, accelerations — ’re just moving camera.  Now Earth sits center, Moon traces familiar near-circular orbit, Sun sweeps wide arc background. geocentric view — system, seen different place.

what: A Binary Star System from the Planet’s View
title: Reference Frames
text: Reference frame shifts aren’t limited massive body. can center body system. ’s Kepler-16b — real circumbinary planet — looking back two parent stars: default (barycentric) frame, see planet’s wide orbit stars’ tight inner dance:  Now shift planet’s perspective:  Kepler-16b, stars trace looping spirograph-like patterns — combination binary’s mutual orbit planet’s revolution around pair. double sunset looks like map time: two stars dance around slowly circling sky.
code: AU <- distance_earth_sun  m_A <- 0.68 * mass_sun m_B <- 0.20 * mass_sun m_planet <- 0.333 * mass_jupiter  a_bin <- 0.22 * AU r_A <- a_bin * m_B / (m_A + m_B) r_B <- a_bin * m_A / (m_A + m_B) v_A <- sqrt(gravitational_constant * m_B^2 / ((m_A + m_B) * a_bin)) v_B <- sqrt(gravitational_constant * m_A^2 / ((m_A + m_B) * a_bin))  r_planet <- 0.7048 * AU v_planet <- sqrt(gravitational_constant * (m_A + m_B) / r_planet)  kepler16 <- create_system() |>   add_body("Star A", mass = m_A, x = r_A, vy = v_A) |>   add_body("Star B", mass = m_B, x = -r_B, vy = -v_B) |>   add_body("Kepler-16b", mass = m_planet, x = r_planet, vy = v_planet) |>   simulate_system(time_step = seconds_per_hour, duration = seconds_per_day * 228.8 * 3) kepler16 |> plot_orbits() kepler16 |>   shift_reference_frame("Kepler-16b", keep_center = FALSE) |>   plot_orbits()

title: The Physics
text: Every body system attracts every body according Newton’s Law Universal Gravitation. body jj, net acceleration due bodies kk : →j=∑k≠jGmkrjk2r̂jk\vec{}_j = \sum_{k \neq j} \frac{G \, m_k}{r_{jk}^2} \, \hat{r}_{jk} rjk=|r→k−r→j|r_{jk} = \lvert\vec{r}_k - \vec{r}_j\rvert distance two bodies r̂jk\hat{r}_{jk} unit vector pointing jj toward kk.

title: The Physics
text: Gravity alone pull every body straight toward every body. prevents colliding initial velocity — sideways motion turns free-fall curved orbit. reason Moon doesn’t crash Earth: ’s falling toward us constantly, ’s also moving sideways fast enough keeps missing. call add_body(), vx, vy, vz parameters set initial velocity. balance speed distance determines shape orbit. given distance rr central mass MM, circular orbit velocity : vcirc=GMrv_{\text{circ}} = \sqrt{\frac{G \, M}{r}} body’s speed exactly matches , traces perfect circle. Faster orbit stretches ellipse (escapes entirely v≥vcirc2v \geq v_{\text{circ}} \sqrt{2}). Slower orbit drops tighter ellipse dips closer central body. zero velocity, body falls straight — orbit .

title: The Physics
text: two bodies pass close, r→0r \0 acceleration diverges toward infinity. well-known numerical problem N-body codes. orbitr offers optional softening length ε\varepsilon regularizes potential: rsoft=r2+ε2r_{\text{soft}} = \sqrt{r^2 + \varepsilon^2} softening enabled, close encounters produce large finite forces instead blowing NaN. Set softening = 0 (default) exact Newtonian gravity, try something like softening = 1e4 (10 km) dense systems.

title: The Physics
text: simulate_system() offers three methods stepping system forward time. operate 3D Cartesian coordinates.

title: The Physics
text: second-order symplectic integrator. conserves energy long timescales, making gold standard orbital mechanics. Orbits stay closed stable indefinitely. x→t+Δt=x→t+v→tΔt+12a→tΔt2\vec{x}_{t+\Delta t} = \vec{x}_t + \vec{v}_t \, \Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2} \vec{}_t \, \Delta t^2 v→t+Δt=v→t+12(→t+→t+Δt)Δt\vec{v}_{t+\Delta t} = \vec{v}_t + \tfrac{1}{2} \left( \vec{}_t + \vec{}_{t+\Delta t} \right) \Delta t position advanced first, acceleration recalculated new position, finally velocity updated using average old new accelerations. requires two acceleration evaluations per step (main cost), payoff stability enormous.

title: The Physics
text: first-order symplectic method. updates velocity first, uses new velocity update position. small reordering prevents systematic energy drift plagues standard Euler: v→t+Δt=v→t+→tΔt\vec{v}_{t+\Delta t} = \vec{v}_t + \vec{}_t \, \Delta t x→t+Δt=x→t+v→t+ΔtΔt\vec{x}_{t+\Delta t} = \vec{x}_t + \vec{v}_{t+\Delta t} \, \Delta t Faster Verlet (one acceleration evaluation per step) less accurate. Good quick previews.

title: The Physics
text: classical textbook method. Position velocity updated using values current time step: x→t+Δt=x→t+v→tΔt\vec{x}_{t+\Delta t} = \vec{x}_t + \vec{v}_t \, \Delta t v→t+Δt=v→t+→tΔt\vec{v}_{t+\Delta t} = \vec{v}_t + \vec{}_t \, \Delta t artificially pumps energy system, causing orbits spiral outward time. Included primarily educational comparison — use Verlet real work.

title: The Physics
text: Verlet traces clean closed ellipse, Euler-Cromer stays close drifts slightly, standard Euler spirals outward pumps energy orbit.
code: library(orbitr) library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: 'dplyr' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union  system <- create_system() |>   add_body("Star", mass = 1e30) |>   add_body("Planet", mass = 1e24, x = 1e11, vy = 30000)  verlet <- simulate_system(system, time_step = seconds_per_hour, duration = seconds_per_year, method = "verlet") |>   mutate(method = "Velocity Verlet")  euler_cromer <- simulate_system(system, time_step = seconds_per_hour, duration = seconds_per_year, method = "euler_cromer") |>   mutate(method = "Euler-Cromer")  euler <- simulate_system(system, time_step = seconds_per_hour, duration = seconds_per_year, method = "euler") |>   mutate(method = "Standard Euler")  bind_rows(verlet, euler_cromer, euler) |>   filter(id == "Planet") |>   ggplot2::ggplot(ggplot2::aes(x = x, y = y, color = method)) +   ggplot2::geom_path(alpha = 0.7) +   ggplot2::coord_equal() +   ggplot2::theme_minimal()

title: The Physics
text: Everything describes orbitr simulates — stepping positions velocities forward using Newton’s law. ’s older, elegant description orbits predates Newton decades: Kepler’s laws. Johannes Kepler showed 1609 planetary orbits ellipses Sun one focus, sweep equal areas equal times. geometric properties lead compact way describing orbit just six numbers, called Keplerian orbital elements. six elements split three groups: Shape — big stretched ellipse? Semi-major axis (aa): half longest diameter ellipse. Determines orbital period Kepler’s Third Law: T=2πa3/μT = 2\pi\sqrt{^3 / \mu}, μ=GM\mu = GM gravitational parameter parent body. Eccentricity (ee): elongated ellipse . e=0e = 0 circle; 0<e<10 < e < 1 ellipse. point, distance parent r=(1−e2)/(1+ecosν)r = (1-e^2)/(1 + e\cos\nu). Orientation — ellipse tilted rotated 3D space? Inclination (ii): angle orbital plane reference plane (ecliptic solar system orbits). 0°0° flat; 90°90° polar orbit. Longitude ascending node (Ω\Omega): direction tilt points. Measured reference direction orbit crosses reference plane going “upward.” Argument periapsis (ω\omega): angle within orbital plane ascending node closest-approach point. Rotates ellipse around parent within plane. Position — body right now? True anomaly (ν\nu): angle periapsis body’s current position along orbit. 0°0° periapsis (closest); 180°180° apoapsis (farthest).

text:
orbitr’s simulation engine works entirely Cartesian coordinates — positions (x,y,z)(x, y, z) velocities (vx,vy,vz)(v_x, v_y, v_z). function add_body_keplerian() converts Keplerian elements Cartesian standard three-step procedure: Step 1: Solve orbit equation orbital plane. distance parent true anomaly ν
u : r=(1−e2)1+ecosνr =  rac{(1-e^2)}{1 + e\cos
u} position perifocal frame (coordinate system x-axis points toward periapsis y-axis 90° ahead direction motion): xpf=rcosν,ypf=rsinνx_{	ext{pf}} = r\cos
u, \quad y_{	ext{pf}} = r\sin
u Step 2: Compute velocity vis-viva relation. specific angular momentum h=μa(1−e2)h = \sqrt{\mu (1 - e^2)} gives velocity components perifocal frame: vx,pf=−μhsinν,vy,pf=μh(e+cosν)v_{x,	ext{pf}} = - rac{\mu}{h}\sin
u, \quad v_{y,	ext{pf}} =  rac{\mu}{h}(e + \cos
u) follow conservation angular momentum (h=rv⊥h = rv_\perp) vis-viva equation v2=μ(2/r−1/)v^2 = \mu(2/r - 1/). Step 3: Rotate inertial frame. Three Euler rotations transform perifocal frame simulation’s inertial frame: R=Rz(−Ω)⋅Rx(−)⋅Rz(−ω)R = R_z(-\Omega) \cdot R_x(-) \cdot R_z(-\omega) sequence first rotates −ω-\omega align periapsis ascending node, tilts −-incline orbital plane, finally rotates −Ω-\Omega orient node line. resulting position velocity added parent body’s state get absolute coordinates.

title: A Tidy Physics Engine for Building and Visualizing Orbital Simulations
text: Tidy output — simulate_system() returns standard tibble (one row per body per time step), ready dplyr, ggplot2, plotly, anything else R ecosystem. Built-physical constants — real-world masses, distances, orbital speeds Sun, eight planets, Moon, don’t look anything . See Physical Constants. C++ engine — compiled Rcpp acceleration kernel handles O(n2)O(n^2) gravity loop, automatic fallback vectorized R compiled code isn’t available. Three integrators — Velocity Verlet (default, symplectic, energy-conserving), Euler-Cromer (fast preview), standard Euler (educational comparison). See Physics. 2D 3D plotting — plot_orbits() returns ggplot planar sims auto-dispatches interactive plotly widget body Z-axis motion. See 3D Plotting. Animations — animate_system() renders orbits GIFs fading trails via gganimate, interactive 3D animations plotly. Reference frame shifting — shift_reference_frame("Earth") re-centers simulation body, turning heliocentric view geocentric one. See Reference Frames.

title: A Tidy Physics Engine for Building and Visualizing Orbital Simulations
text: Kepler-16b orbits two stars — real-life Tatooine. orbitr handles multi-body gravitational interactions natively, special setup needed:
code: G  <- gravitational_constant AU <- distance_earth_sun  m_A <- 0.68 * mass_sun m_B <- 0.20 * mass_sun a_bin <- 0.22 * AU  r_A <- a_bin * m_B / (m_A + m_B) r_B <- a_bin * m_A / (m_A + m_B) v_A <- sqrt(G * m_B^2 / ((m_A + m_B) * a_bin)) v_B <- sqrt(G * m_A^2 / ((m_A + m_B) * a_bin))  r_planet <- 0.7048 * AU v_planet <- sqrt(G * (m_A + m_B) / r_planet)  create_system() |>   add_body("Star A",      mass = m_A,                 x = r_A,      vy = v_A) |>   add_body("Star B",      mass = m_B,                 x = -r_B,     vy = -v_B) |>   add_body("Kepler-16b",  mass = 0.333 * mass_jupiter, x = r_planet, vy = v_planet) |>   simulate_system(time_step = seconds_per_hour, duration = seconds_per_day * 228.8 * 3) |>   animate_system(fps = 15, duration = 6)

what: Add a body using Keplerian orbital elements — add_body_keplerian
title: Add a body using Keplerian orbital elements — add_body_keplerian
text: convenience wrapper around [add_body()] lets specify orbit using classical Keplerian elements instead raw Cartesian state vectors. elements converted position velocity reference frame parent body, must already exist system.

title: Add a body using Keplerian orbital elements — add_body_keplerian
code: add_body_keplerian(   system,   id,   mass,   a,   e = 0,   i = 0,   lan = 0,   arg_pe = 0,   nu = 0,   parent )

title: Add a body using Keplerian orbital elements — add_body_keplerian
text: system `orbit_system` object created [create_system()]. id unique character string identify body. mass mass body kilograms. Semi-major axis meters. e Eccentricity (0 = circle, 0 < e < 1 = ellipse). Default 0. Inclination degrees. Default 0. lan Longitude ascending node degrees. Default 0. arg_pe Argument periapsis degrees. Default 0. nu True anomaly degrees. Default 0 (body starts periapsis). parent Character id parent body (must already exist `system`). orbital elements defined relative body.

title: Add a body using Keplerian orbital elements — add_body_keplerian
text: updated `orbit_system` new body added.

title: Add a body using Keplerian orbital elements — add_body_keplerian
text: Six numbers fully describe Keplerian orbit: `` (semi-major axis) size orbit — half longest     diameter ellipse, meters. `e` (eccentricity) shape orbit. 0 perfect circle;     values 0 1 ellipses. `` (inclination) tilt orbital plane relative     reference plane, degrees. `lan` (longitude ascending node) angle reference     direction orbit crosses reference plane going "upward,"     degrees. Sometimes written \(\Omega\). `arg_pe` (argument periapsis) angle within orbital     plane ascending node closest-approach point, degrees.     Sometimes written \(\omega\). `nu` (true anomaly) body currently sits along orbit,     measured angle periapsis degrees. 0 = periapsis     (closest), 180 = apoapsis (farthest).

title: Add a body using Keplerian orbital elements — add_body_keplerian
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Earth orbiting the Sun with real orbital elements system <- create_system() |>   add_body("Sun", mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body_keplerian(     "Earth", mass = mass_earth,     a = distance_earth_sun, e = 0.0167, i = 0.00005,     parent = "Sun"   )  # Mars with its notable eccentricity system <- system |>   add_body_keplerian(     "Mars", mass = mass_mars,     a = distance_mars_sun, e = 0.0934, i = 1.85,     lan = 49.6, arg_pe = 286.5, nu = 0,     parent = "Sun"   ) } # }

what: Add a known solar system body by name — add_planet
title: Add a known solar system body by name — add_planet

code: add_planet(   system,   name,   parent,   nu = 0,   a = NULL,   e = NULL,   i = NULL,   lan = NULL,   arg_pe = NULL,   mass = NULL )

title: Add a known solar system body by name — add_planet
text: system `orbit_system` object. name name body. Must one : `"Mercury"`, `"Venus"`, `"Earth"`, `"Mars"`, `"Jupiter"`, `"Saturn"`, `"Uranus"`, `"Neptune"`, `"Moon"`, `"Pluto"`. Case-sensitive. parent Character id parent body, must already exist system. planets Pluto typically `"Sun"`; Moon `"Earth"`. nu True anomaly degrees (default 0, body starts periapsis). commonly overridden element — use spread planets around orbits instead starting periapsis. Override semi-major axis (meters). e Override eccentricity. Override inclination (degrees). lan Override longitude ascending node (degrees). arg_pe Override argument periapsis (degrees). mass Override mass (kg).

title: Animate the System Over Time (Smart 2D/3D Dispatch) — animate_system

title: Physical Constants for Orbital Mechanics — physical_constants
text: `gravitational_constant`: Newton's gravitational constant   (6.6743 x 10^-11 m^3 kg^-1 s^-2). Source: CODATA 2018 recommended value.   Use `create_system()` scale gravity:   `create_system(G = gravitational_constant * 10)`. `seconds_per_hour`: 3,600 seconds. Convenient setting `time_step`   lunar close-orbit simulations. `seconds_per_day`: 86,400 seconds. Convenient setting `time_step`   planetary-scale simulations. `seconds_per_year`: 31,557,600 seconds (365.25 days, Julian year).   Convenient setting `duration` `simulate_system()`. `mass_sun`: Mass Sun (1.989 x 10^30 kg). Source: IAU 2015 nominal solar mass. `mass_earth`: Mass Earth (5.972 x 10^24 kg). Source: IAU 2015 nominal Earth mass. `mass_moon`: Mass Moon (7.342 x 10^22 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_mars`: Mass Mars (6.417 x 10^23 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_jupiter`: Mass Jupiter (1.898 x 10^27 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_saturn`: Mass Saturn (5.683 x 10^26 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_venus`: Mass Venus (4.867 x 10^24 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_mercury`: Mass Mercury (3.301 x 10^23 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_uranus`: Mass Uranus (8.681 x 10^25 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_neptune`: Mass Neptune (1.024 x 10^26 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_pluto`: Mass Pluto (1.309 x 10^22 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris.   Pluto dwarf planet included convenience. `distance_earth_sun`: Semi-major axis Earth's orbit around Sun   (1.496 x 10^11 m, ~149.6 million km). Earth's actual distance varies   ~147.1 million km (perihelion) ~152.1 million km (aphelion). `distance_earth_moon`: Semi-major axis Moon's orbit around Earth   (3.844 x 10^8 m, ~384,400 km). Moon's actual distance varies   ~363,300 km (perigee) ~405,500 km (apogee). `distance_mars_sun`: Semi-major axis Mars's orbit around Sun   (2.279 x 10^11 m, ~227.9 million km). Mars notably eccentric orbit   (e = 0.093), ranging ~206.7 million km ~249.2 million km. `distance_jupiter_sun`: Semi-major axis Jupiter's orbit around Sun   (7.785 x 10^11 m, ~778.5 million km). `distance_venus_sun`: Semi-major axis Venus's orbit around Sun   (1.082 x 10^11 m, ~108.2 million km). Venus circular orbit   planet (e = 0.007). `distance_mercury_sun`: Semi-major axis Mercury's orbit around Sun   (5.791 x 10^10 m, ~57.9 million km). Mercury eccentric planetary   orbit (e = 0.206), ranging ~46.0 million km ~69.8 million km. `distance_saturn_sun`: Semi-major axis Saturn's orbit around Sun   (1.434 x 10^12 m, ~1.434 billion km). `distance_uranus_sun`: Semi-major axis Uranus's orbit around Sun   (2.871 x 10^12 m, ~2.871 billion km). `distance_neptune_sun`: Semi-major axis Neptune's orbit around Sun   (4.495 x 10^12 m, ~4.495 billion km). `distance_pluto_sun`: Semi-major axis Pluto's orbit around Sun   (5.906 x 10^12 m, ~5.906 billion km). Pluto highly eccentric orbit   (e = 0.249), ranging ~4.437 billion km ~7.376 billion km. `speed_earth`: Mean orbital speed Earth around Sun (29,780 m/s). `speed_moon`: Mean orbital speed Moon around Earth (1,022 m/s). `speed_mars`: Mean orbital speed Mars around Sun (24,070 m/s). `speed_jupiter`: Mean orbital speed Jupiter around Sun (13,060 m/s). `speed_venus`: Mean orbital speed Venus around Sun (35,020 m/s). `speed_mercury`: Mean orbital speed Mercury around Sun (47,360 m/s). `speed_saturn`: Mean orbital speed Saturn around Sun (9,680 m/s). `speed_uranus`: Mean orbital speed Uranus around Sun (6,800 m/s). `speed_neptune`: Mean orbital speed Neptune around Sun (5,430 m/s). `speed_pluto`: Mean orbital speed Pluto around Sun (4,740 m/s).

title: Physical Constants for Orbital Mechanics — physical_constants
text: Orbital distances truly constant. Every orbit ellipse, separation two bodies changes continuously. distances provided **semi-major axes** — average closest approach (periapsis) farthest point (apoapsis). semi-major axis single characteristic length scale elliptical orbit: determines orbital period via Kepler's Third Law, paired circular velocity distance, produces near-circular orbit closely approximates real trajectory. example, Earth-Sun distance varies 147.1 million km (perihelion January) 152.1 million km (aphelion July). semi-major axis 149.598 million km sits right middle gives correct orbital period one year.

what: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate_system
title: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate_system
text: Propagates physical state `orbit_system` time using numerical integration. engine supports multiple mathematical methods, defaulting energy-conserving Velocity Verlet algorithm ensure highly stable orbital trajectories.

title: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate_system
code: simulate_system(   system,   time_step = seconds_per_hour,   duration = seconds_per_year,   method = "verlet",   softening = 0,   use_cpp = TRUE )

title: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate_system
text: system `orbit_system` object created `create_system()`. time_step time increment per frame seconds (default 3600s / 1 hour). planetary orbits around star, daily steps (`86400`) usually sufficient. lunar-scale tighter orbits, hourly steps (`3600`) work well. duration Total simulation time seconds (default 31557600s / 1 year). method numerical integration method: "verlet" (default), "euler_cromer", "euler". softening small distance (meters) added prevent numerical singularities bodies pass close . gravitational distance computed `sqrt(r^2 + softening^2)` instead `r`. Default 0 (softening). value like 1e4 (10 km) reasonable planetary simulations. use_cpp Logical. `TRUE` (default), uses compiled C++ acceleration engine better performance. Falls back vectorized R C++ code available.

title: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate_system
text: tidy `tibble` containing physical state (time, id, mass, x, y, z, vx, vy, vz)   every body every time step.

title: orbitr 0.2.0
text: add_body_keplerian(): Add body system using classical Keplerian orbital elements (semi-major axis, eccentricity, inclination, longitude ascending node, argument periapsis, true anomaly) instead raw Cartesian state vectors. elements converted position velocity relative specified parent body. add_planet(): Add known solar system body name real orbital data looked automatically. Just specify name parent — mass, semi-major axis, eccentricity, inclination, orientation filled JPL DE440 values. element can overridden “” scenarios (e.g., add_planet("Mars", parent = "Sun", e = 0) circular Mars). load_solar_system(): One-liner builds complete solar system — Sun, eight planets, Moon, optionally Pluto — using real orbital data JPL DE440 ephemeris. Returns ready--simulate orbit_system. Use moon = FALSE pluto = FALSE exclude bodies.

title: orbitr 0.2.0
text: three_d parameter plot_orbits(), plot_system(), animate_system() now supports FALSE force 2D output even data Z-axis motion. Previously, three_d = FALSE default override auto-detection. Fixed frozen 3D animations animate_system_3d() caused redraw = FALSE plotly animation options.

title: orbitr 0.1.0
text: Initial release.

title: orbitr 0.1.0
text: N-body gravitational simulation three integration methods: Velocity Verlet (default), Euler-Cromer, standard Euler. Compiled C++ acceleration engine via Rcpp automatic fallback vectorized R. Optional gravitational softening prevent singularities close approach.

title: orbitr 0.1.0
text: create_system() / add_body() / simulate_system() pipe-friendly API. Tidy tibble output (one row per body per time step) use dplyr, ggplot2, plotly, etc. shift_reference_frame() re-center simulations body.

title: orbitr 0.1.0
text: plot_orbits() / plot_orbits_3d() quick trajectory plots (ggplot2 2D, plotly 3D). plot_system() / plot_system_3d() single-time-step snapshots. animate_system() / animate_system_3d() animated orbits via gganimate plotly. Automatic 2D/3D dispatch based whether body non-zero Z motion.

title: orbitr 0.1.0
text: Real-world masses, orbital distances, speeds Sun, eight planets, Moon. gravitational_constant scaling gravity create_system(). seconds_per_hour, seconds_per_day, seconds_per_year time helpers.
